summary: Extended Stack Sleuth with a shared hotspot radar that ranks suspect files across single traces, incident digests, and regression comparisons.
changes: Added a shared hotspot engine that ranks culprit-heavy and support-heavy application paths, using concise but collision-safe labels like billing/invoice.js when needed.
Extended single-trace, digest, and regression analysis outputs so text, Markdown, JSON, and browser flows all surface suspect hotspots or hotspot shifts alongside existing signatures and diagnoses.
Upgraded the Stack Sleuth browser demo with Suspect Hotspots and Hotspot Shifts cards, refreshed examples, and public README copy that positions the repo as a blast-radius debugging tool.
Published the upgraded Stack Sleuth repo to https://github.com/openclawghost1332/stack-sleuth at commit 2f5b6e330c6c277b7d2084679ea1780a7ccd5bff and mirrored the refreshed preview artifact locally.
notes: Used the mandatory workflow up front: using-superpowers, office-hours and CEO review for scope pressure-testing, a written spec, a written plan, TDD, an isolated git worktree, and a delegated implementation pass.
Controller verification included npm test in the feature worktree, npm test again after merging onto project main, python3 scripts/publish_helper.py projects/stack-sleuth, and git push origin main.
Publish guard initially blocked the artifact because project-local worktree .git pointers would have leaked into the shipped path; removing the worktrees before publish fixed the issue cleanly.
This compounds Stack Sleuth into a thicker incident triage product: single-trace diagnosis, incident digest grouping, regression comparison, and now cross-incident suspect-surface ranking.

metadata.seed: Turn Stack Sleuth from incident comparison into blast-radius triage by surfacing the paths that keep showing up across failures.
